title: Chapter 15 Quiz: Advanced Topics & Security
description: Test your knowledge of advanced PHP features and security!

questions.question: Which function converts a PHP array to a JSON string?
questions.explanation: json_encode() converts PHP data to JSON.

questions.question: json_decode() with second parameter true returns an associative array.
questions.explanation: Yes, passing true as second argument returns arrays instead of objects.

questions.question: What does the DateTime class method modify() do?
options: Changes the date/time relative to current value
questions.explanation: modify() alters the date by a relative string (e.g., '+1 day').

questions.question: Which method returns a DateInterval representing the difference between two DateTime objects?
questions.explanation: diff() returns the difference as a DateInterval.

questions.question: The mail() function can send emails with attachments directly.
questions.explanation: mail() is basic; for attachments you'd need to craft MIME manually or use a library.

questions.question: Which cURL option returns the response as a string?
questions.explanation: CURLOPT_RETURNTRANSFER makes curl_exec return the response instead of outputting it.

questions.question: What is SQL injection?
options: Inserting malicious SQL code into user input to manipulate queries
A method to speed up queries
questions.explanation: SQL injection is a code injection technique that exploits security vulnerabilities in an application's database layer.

questions.question: Prepared statements with PDO prevent SQL injection.
questions.explanation: Yes, prepared statements separate SQL logic from data, preventing injection.

questions.question: Which function is used to prevent XSS when outputting user data?
questions.explanation: htmlspecialchars() converts special characters to HTML entities, preventing XSS.

questions.question: What is a CSRF token?
options: A unique token used to verify that requests come from the legitimate site
questions.explanation: CSRF tokens are used to validate that the request was intentionally made by the user.

questions.question: password_hash() automatically generates a secure salt.
questions.explanation: Yes, password_hash() generates a strong random salt and includes it in the hash.

questions.question: Which function checks if a password matches a hash?
questions.explanation: password_verify() compares a password with a hash.

questions.question: When uploading files, why should you not trust the file's MIME type from $_FILES?
options: It can be easily spoofed by an attacker
questions.explanation: The client can send any MIME type; use server-side detection (finfo).

questions.question: You should store uploaded files with their original filenames to avoid confusion.
questions.explanation: Rename uploaded files to random names to prevent directory traversal and collisions.

questions.question: What does json_last_error_msg() return?
options: The last error message from JSON encoding/decoding
The last error from file uploads
questions.explanation: It returns the last error message from a JSON operation.

questions.question: Which PHP function can be used to generate a cryptographically secure random string for CSRF tokens?
questions.explanation: random_bytes() generates cryptographically secure random bytes.

questions.question: The CURLOPT_POSTFIELDS option can accept an array for POST data.
questions.explanation: If given an array, cURL will send it as multipart/form-data; if a string, as application/x-www-form-urlencoded.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'cost' option in password_hash()?
options: It determines the computational cost of the hash (slower is more secure)
questions.explanation: Higher cost makes hashing slower, increasing resistance to brute-force attacks.

questions.question: Which header can help protect against XSS by instructing the browser to block reflected XSS?
questions.explanation: X-XSS-Protection (deprecated) and Content-Security-Policy both help mitigate XSS.

questions.question: You should always store passwords in plain text for performance.
questions.explanation: Never store passwords in plain text; always hash them securely.
